items.myth: Softmax outputs the correct class probability with certainty when the model is right.
items.reality: Softmax always spreads probability mass across all classes. Even on easy examples, π_y is rarely exactly 1 unless one logit dominates by a huge margin. Calibration requires separate techniques.
items.example: Logits [3, 1, 1] give π ≈ [0.84, 0.08, 0.08] — correct argmax but not 100% confidence.

items.myth: Softmax and sigmoid do the same job for K classes.
items.reality: Sigmoid applies independently per output (K Bernoullis). Softmax couples all K logits so probabilities compete and sum to 1 — appropriate only for mutually exclusive single-label problems.
items.example: Three-class MNIST digit: softmax. Three independent defect flags: three sigmoids, not softmax.

items.myth: You must compute exp explicitly to get softmax probabilities.
items.reality: For training, log_softmax plus negative log-likelihood is preferred and mathematically equivalent. Log-sum-exp avoids ever materializing tiny probabilities.
items.example: F.cross_entropy(logits, y) never forms π then log π; it works entirely in log space.
